steps.title: Identify the revelation about the story's origins
steps.content: Text 2 reveals that despite “no contemporary evidence” of audience alarm, the story persists because “our belief that the coming of film was transformative is so strong that we invented and keep retelling a founding myth.” This single observation shows the story is motivated by perception of significance rather than by factual evidence.

steps.title: Match the author's insight to the choices
steps.content:
Text 2's author argues that the story is invented because of our strong belief in film's significance, not because of evidence.

Choice A directly captures this: the description is “more motivated by a perception of the significance of the invention of film than by facts.” The other three fail: D suggests the myth arose from actual audience alarm, contradicting Text 2's point about the lack of evidence; B and C misread the naïve/sophisticated language as critiquing the transformative view itself, when it actually describes the myth's narrative structure.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
